topic: Two coworkers discover they went to the same summer camp
raw_output:
[story 1]

Eliza tapped her pen against the laminated sign, her jaw tight. "Are you absolutely certain about this, Mark?" she asked, her voice pitched just a little too high. She leaned in, scrutinizing the faded lettering of the camp logo. Mark, across the desk, shifted uncomfortably in his chair, avoiding her gaze. He fiddled with the edge of his notepad, a nervous habit that made Eliza’s own shoulders tense. "Positive," he mumbled, running a hand through his already messy hair. "The dates match. The cabin numbers... they're identical." A slow, unwelcome warmth began to creep up Eliza's neck. She felt a sudden, intense need to dissect every detail, to prove this was a coincidence, a mistake. "It can't be," she insisted, pushing the folder back across the desk with unnecessary force. "We've been at this for three weeks. It's impossible."

[story 2]

"This is absurd," thought Ben, his knuckles white where he gripped the coffee mug. He stared at the grainy photograph on his screen, the smiling faces of two very different versions of himself from years ago staring back. He took a deep, shaky breath, trying to steady the tremor in his hands. "I've checked every record. The registration forms, the staff rosters... everything lines up perfectly," he stated, his tone flat, almost challenging the very evidence before him. Sarah looked up from her keyboard, a flicker of surprise crossing her face. "Wait, what are you talking about?" Ben didn't look at her, his focus locked on the screen. "The summer camp," he finally said, the words clipped. "We went to the same camp."

[story 3]

"Look, I’ve already looked into this," Clara said, crossing her arms tightly over her chest. Her gaze was unwavering, a fixed point of determination. "There are countless summer camps nationwide. The probability of this being a fluke is statistically negligible. We need to re-examine the data, look for an error in transcription." Across the small table, David leaned back, a slow, deliberate sigh escaping him. He didn't meet her eyes immediately, instead tracing the rim of his water glass with his index finger. "I understand your insistence, Clara," he said, his voice low and even. "But I’ve checked the records too. It seems the overlap is undeniable."
